title: Mean Average Precision (mAP)
concept: Mean Average Precision (mAP) is the average of the AP scores across all object classes, serving as the primary metric for detectors.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does AP measure for a single object class?

options.text: The area under its Precision-Recall curve
The number of anchor boxes matched to it
The loss value after one epoch

exercises.explain: AP summarizes class-specific precision and recall as the area under the curve.

cards.title: The Grade Point Average
cards.body: If you take classes in Math, History, and Science, you get separate grades for each. But when you apply to college, they look at your Grade Point Average (GPA) — a single number that averages your grades across all subjects. Mean Average Precision is the GPA of your object detector.

cards.title: Mean Average Precision
cards.body: Mean Average Precision (mAP) is the primary metric used to evaluate object detection models. It is calculated by taking the arithmetic mean of the Average Precision (AP) scores across all object classes in the dataset.

cards.title: School Report Card
cards.body: If the model's report card says: Cat AP = 0.8, Dog AP = 0.6, and Car AP = 0.7, the principal averages these three scores to find the school's overall rating: (0.8 + 0.6 + 0.7) / 3 = 0.70 mAP.

cards.title: IoU Benchmarks
cards.body: mAP depends on the IoU threshold used. mAP@0.5 evaluates boxes at IoU threshold 0.5. mAP@0.5:0.95 calculates the average mAP at 10 different thresholds (0.50, 0.55, ..., 0.95) to grade how tightly the boxes fit.

cards.title: Not a simple accuracy percentage
cards.body: It's a myth that 70% mAP means the model got 70% of the images right. Because it factors in precision, recall, thresholds, and multiple classes, mAP is a much richer metric. A model with 70% mAP is actually highly accurate in practice.

cards.title: Averaging the Classes
cards.body: Visualizing how class APs combine into a single mAP score:

exercises.prompt: How is Mean Average Precision (mAP) calculated?
exercises.explain: mAP is the mathematical average (mean) of the individual AP scores calculated for each class.

options.text: By calculating the average of the AP scores across all object classes
By multiplying the classification loss by the regression loss
By counting the total number of True Positives
By averaging the pixel values of the bounding boxes

exercises.prompt: True or False: A model's mAP score is independent of the IoU threshold used during evaluation.
exercises.explain: False. mAP is highly dependent on the IoU threshold; a higher threshold (like 0.75) requires tighter box fits, resulting in a lower mAP.
The suffix @0.5 specifies that bounding boxes with IoU >= 0.5 are counted as True Positives.
exercises.sentence: The notation mAP@0.5 means the metric is evaluated using an IoU threshold of ___.

exercises.prompt: Which description represents a model's overall evaluation metric score?
exercises.explain: The mAP@0.5:0.95 score represents the standard overall evaluation metric for object detection.

options.text: mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.48 (averaged across 10 overlap thresholds and all classes)
Learning Rate = 0.001 (configured for the optimizer backbone)

exercises.prompt: You are completing your evaluation of classical and early deep learning object detectors. You have mastered classification, localization, anchor boxes, selective search, R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN, and evaluation metrics like mAP. What is the overall status of your foundations?

options.text: Phase 7: Object Detection Foundations is complete. Next up: Modern Object Detection.
The model has trained for 10 epochs and needs to run on CPU.
We need to go back and learn about linear regression.
The training pipeline has crashed due to class imbalance.

exercises.explain: Phase 7: Object Detection Foundations is complete. Next up: Modern Object Detection.
